docs.title: Home
docs.text: Mengke Zhang  PhD, Architecture and Sciences of the City · EPFL   I study the political economy of regional development, periphery urbanization, and infrastructure-led urban transformation in China. I use ethnographic and qualitative methods to examine how state strategy, mega-events, and financialized development reshape territories, institutions, and everyday life. My PhD investigated the mechanisms of extended urbanization driven by the 2022 Beijing Winter Olympics.  Publications Research About

docs.title: About Me
docs.text:
I am a researcher at the intersection of urban planning, urban studies, and human geography. My work examines the political economy of regional development, peri-urban and marginal areas, infrastructure-led spatial transformation, and the politics of space under state-led development.

I recently completed my PhD at the Heritage, Anthropology and Technologies Research Group, École Polytechnique Fédérale de Lausanne (EPFL), Switzerland. My dissertation, Reconfiguring the Mountains: Mechanisms and Practices of Chongli's Extended Urbanization Driven by the 2022 Beijing Winter Olympics, explored how mega-events and state strategy reshape mountain regions through financialization, infrastructural development, and socio-spatial transformation. The research was grounded in seven months of ethnographic fieldwork in Chongli, Hebei, involving over 80 in-depth interviews with government officials, state-owned enterprise managers, resort operators, and local residents.

My research interests sit at the intersection of regional political economy and spatial governance, extended urbanization and marginal area development, infrastructure and state-led regional transformation, urban-rural integration and social change, and urban heritage and sustainable regeneration. I work primarily with qualitative and ethnographic methods — fieldwork, semi-structured interviews, participant observation, and policy and discourse analysis — complemented by spatial analysis using ArcGIS.

How I Built This Website with Claude Code :calendar: May 29 · :label: ClaudeCode PersonalWebsite AcademicPortfolio

Building a bilingual academic homepage from scratch with AI-assisted development — the tech stack, the workflow, the bugs, and what surprised me along the way. Now live with research projects, publications, an interactive map, and this blog.

Read more →

docs.title: How I Built This Website with Claude Code
docs.text:
May 29, 2026 · Tags: ClaudeCode PersonalWebsite AcademicPortfolio

The Chinese original of this post is available here.

I've been meaning to set up a personal academic homepage for a while. Like many researchers, I wanted something clean, bilingual, and easy to maintain—but I kept putting it off. The options all felt like compromises: WordPress required too much upkeep, Notion locked you into its ecosystem, and hand-coding from scratch felt like a weekend I didn't have.

Then I tried Claude Code—the tool you're interacting with right now.

docs.title: The Setup
docs.text:
I dropped my CV (a Markdown file) into an empty project folder and asked: What kind of project should we build?

It read my CV, understood my background in urban studies, and suggested four ideas. I picked building a personal academic website—practical, visible, and a good test of the collaborative workflow.

docs.title: The Tech Stack
docs.text:
We settled on Material for MkDocs:

  • Content in Markdown—I already write everything in .md, so no new skills needed
  • Bilingual from day one—English and Chinese, with a one-click language switcher
  • Zero lock-in—plain .md files, no database, no proprietary format. If I want to switch to another tool later, my content moves with me
  • Free hosting—via GitHub Pages, no domain costs required

docs.title: The Workflow
docs.text:
We worked in tight feedback loops:

  1. I described what I wanted (in Chinese—my native language for thinking)
  2. Claude proposed a plan with trade-offs
  3. I approved or redirected
  4. Claude wrote the code while I watched
  5. A live preview at localhost:8000 let me see results instantly
  6. I gave feedback, and we iterated

docs.title: What's Live Now
docs.text:
As of today, the site includes:

  • Homepage—bilingual, with a clean researcher profile and research interests
  • Publications—6 journal articles + 2 book chapters + manuscripts in preparation, all with clickable DOIs
  • Research Projects—5 dedicated project pages covering my PhD (Chongli's extended urbanization), Beijing 2022 cultural heritage, platform-mediated live music, danwei spatial transformation, and Grand Canal heritage conservation
  • Blog—this very page. We ditched Material's built-in blog plugin (it doesn't play well with the i18n plugin—took a while to debug that one) and went with hand-written markdown pages instead. Simpler, more controllable
  • Interactive Map—Leaflet.js + OpenStreetMap, with pins on my four fieldwork sites (Chongli, Beijing, Hangzhou, Shenzhen), each with a popup description
  • Dark mode—one-click toggle for late-night reading

docs.title: What Surprised Me
docs.text:
• The AI read my context. I didn't have to repeat my background or goals. Claude read my CV and understood my field well enough to suggest relevant project ideas.
  • I could stay in flow. No context-switching to Google for "how to configure mkdocs i18n." Claude either knew it or read the source code of the plugins on the fly.
  • Everything is in Git. All content, all config, all design decisions documented in SITE-PLAN.md. The project is fully portable.
  • It remembers across sessions. My preferences, project setup, and design choices were stored so future sessions pick up where we left off.
  • It reads academic papers. I dropped my published PDFs into the project folder, and Claude extracted the research questions, methods, and key findings from each one to draft project pages. I reviewed and refined—but the heavy lifting was done.

docs.title: Bugs Are Part of the Learning
docs.text:
We hit a few bumps along the way:

  • Blog plugin vs. i18n incompatibility—the listing page rendered completely blank. Took several mkdocs serve restarts to pinpoint the issue. Eventually ditched the plugin and went with hand-written markdown. Cleaner solution anyway.
  • 404 on language switch—the English alternate link was set to /en/, but since English is the default language it builds to /. A one-line fix.
  • Translation inconsistencies—pages initially translated from Chinese had terminology errors (e.g., "Laboratory" instead of "Research Group," "Architecture and Urban Sciences" instead of "Architecture and Sciences of the City"). Once I added my English CV to the project folder, Claude cross-checked everything and fixed it systematically.

docs.title: What's Next
docs.text:
• Deploy the site live
  • Keep blogging here
  • Maybe add a custom domain down the line

docs.title: Verdict
docs.text:
Was it perfect? No—there were moments of misalignment, a 404 bug, CSS that needed tuning, and an hour lost to a plugin compatibility issue. But the pace and quality were surprising for a first encounter with this kind of tool.

For researchers who want a web presence without becoming a web developer: this workflow is worth trying.

Originally written in Chinese. Translated by the author with AI assistance.

docs.title: Research
docs.text: My research sits at the intersection of urban political economy, spatial governance, and extended urbanization, with a regional focus on China. I use qualitative and ethnographic methods to examine how state-led development reshapes territories, institutions, and everyday life.

docs.title: Chongli's Extended Urbanization
docs.text: **2020–2025** · PhD Dissertation, EPFL How did the 2022 Beijing Winter Olympics transform a poor mountain county into China's first Olympic mountain city? This project traces the financial, infrastructural, and social mechanisms behind Chongli's rapid urbanization. Extended UrbanizationTerritorial Business ModelState Developmental Urbanism

docs.title: Cultural Heritage at Beijing 2022
docs.text: **2022–2024** · SNSF Research Project Part of an international, interdisciplinary team examining how the Beijing 2022 Winter Olympics mobilized cultural heritage — from industrial sites to intangible practices — for state-led urban regeneration and nation-branding. Industrial HeritageMega-EventsUrban Regeneration

docs.title: Platform-Mediated Live Music and Urban Spaces
docs.text: **2018–2020** · Peking University Shenzhen How do digital platforms reshape live music performance and create new urban cultural spaces in Chinese cities? A spatial analysis of an emerging cultural economy. Platform EconomyCultural SpacesLive Music

docs.title: Property Rights and the Danwei Compound
docs.text: **2018–2020** · Peking University Shenzhen Tracing how property rights redistribution has driven the spatial and institutional transformation of Beijing's socialist danwei (work-unit) compounds since market reforms. Property RightsDanweiInstitutional Change

docs.title: Conservation of the Grand Canal
docs.text: **2015–2016** · MA Thesis, HKU Examining the mismatch between official heritage discourse and local realities in the conservation of China's Grand Canal — a case study of the Hangzhou section. Authorized Heritage DiscourseCanal ConservationUrban China

docs.title: Cultural Heritage at the Beijing 2022 Winter Olympics
docs.text: 2022–2024 · SNSF Research Project · EPFL

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Part of the SNSF-funded international research project "Uses of Cultural Heritage at the Beijing Winter Olympic Games of 2022," this research examines how China's first Winter Olympics mobilized cultural heritage — both industrial and intangible — for urban regeneration and nation-branding.

Working within a cross-disciplinary team spanning geography, architecture, anthropology, and sociology, the project explored the creative and sometimes contradictory ways heritage was defined, preserved, and repurposed in the service of the Olympic spectacle.

docs.title: Research Focus
docs.text:
• How does the Chinese state adopt a state-led approach to industrial heritage, distinct from Western market-driven or civil-society-led models?
  • What role does heritage play in capital accumulation and urban regeneration in the context of mega-events?
  • How are competing narratives of heritage — local, national, global — negotiated in Olympic-driven development?

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text: The Beijing 2022 case reveals a distinctive state-led model where industrial heritage preservation is instrumentalized simultaneously for capital accumulation, urban regeneration, and heritage preservation — three goals often in tension in other contexts.

docs.title: Outputs
docs.text:
• Zhang, M. (2024). The state-led approach to industrial heritage in China's mega-events. Built Heritage, 8(30). [DOI]
  • Roux, H. & Zhang, M. (2026). "Mega-events and the creative use of cultural heritage." In P. Larsen & F. Graezer Bideau (Eds.), The Heritage, Creativity and Innovation Nexus. Routledge.

docs.title: Reconfiguring the Mountains: Chongli's Extended Urbanization
docs.text: 2020–2025 · PhD Dissertation · EPFL

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This project investigates how the 2022 Beijing Winter Olympics catalyzed the rapid transformation of Chongli — a former poverty-stricken mountain county 200 km from Beijing — into China's first Olympic mountain city. The research sits at the intersection of mega-events, city-region development, and a new paradigm of urbanization in mountain peripheries.

Drawing on seven months of ethnographic fieldwork conducted between late 2021 and 2024, the study traces Chongli's urbanization through three interconnected dimensions: the political-economic configurations underpinning mountain development, the evolution of ski resorts as pivotal social infrastructure, and the extension of Olympic momentum into rural spaces.

docs.title: Research Questions
docs.text:
• What institutional, financial, and political mechanisms drive mountain urbanization in the context of a mega-event?
  • How do ski resorts function as both consumption enclaves for urbanites and livelihood resources for local communities?
  • How does Olympic-driven restructuring reconfigure rural economic networks, social relations, and residents' livelihoods?

docs.title: Methods
docs.text:
• 7 months of ethnographic fieldwork in Chongli (2021–2024)
  • 80+ semi-structured interviews with government officials, SOE managers, resort operators, and local residents
  • Longitudinal dataset of policy archives, planning documents, and investment materials

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
State Developmental Urbanism — a framework introduced in the thesis to characterize the dominant role of the Chinese state in shaping mountain urbanization through development and urban planning policies.

Territorial Business Model — the institutional, financial, and political arrangements that transform a territory into an investable asset, linking land rent production to Olympic-driven financialization.

docs.title: Outputs
docs.text:
• Zhang, M. (2025). PhD Dissertation, EPFL. [infoscience]
  • Theurillat, T. & Zhang, M. (2025). Financialized green state entrepreneurialism and the urbanization of mountains. IJURR. [DOI]
  • Zhang, M. "Unraveling the relational production of spatial rent" (in preparation)
  • Zhang, M. "Infrastructuralizing the periphery" (in preparation)

docs.title: Property Rights and Spatial Evolution of the Chinese Danwei
docs.text: 2018–2020 · Peking University Shenzhen Graduate School

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The danwei (work-unit) compound was the basic spatial and social unit of urban China during the planned economy era. Since market reforms, these compounds have undergone profound transformation. This project traces how the redistribution of property rights has reshaped danwei spaces, institutions, and communities in Beijing.

docs.title: Research Questions
docs.text:
• How has property rights redistribution driven the spatial evolution of danwei compounds?
  • What institutional mechanisms mediate between property rights changes and spatial outcomes?
  • How do residents and work-units negotiate the transformation of inherited socialist spaces?

docs.title: Empirical Case
docs.text: Field research in Beijing examined danwei compounds as microcosms of broader urban transformation in China. By tracing specific compounds' spatial and institutional trajectories, the research reveals how the legacy of the socialist planned economy continues to shape contemporary urban form.

docs.title: Outputs
docs.text:
• Zhang, M., Zhang, T., Xiao, Z., & Chai, Y. (2021). Property rights redistribution and the spatial evolution of the Chinese danwei compound. Journal of Housing and the Built Environment, 36(4), 1585–1602. [DOI]
  • Xiao, Z., Liu, T., Chai, Y., & Zhang, M. (2020). Corporate-run society: The practice of the danwei system in Beijing during the planned economy period. Sustainability, 12(4), 1338. [DOI]
  • Xiao, Z., Liu, T., Chai, Y., & Zhang, M. (2020). Rethinking the spatial prototype and operational organization of the Chinese danwei system. In S. Jacoby & J. Cheng (Eds.), The Socio-spatial Design of Community and Governance (pp. 79–86). Springer.

docs.title: Conservation of the Grand Canal in Urban China
docs.text: 2015–2016 · MA Thesis · The University of Hong Kong

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This project, developed from my MA thesis at HKU, examines the mismatch between official heritage frameworks and local realities in the conservation of China's Grand Canal — a UNESCO World Heritage site stretching over 1,700 km. Focusing on the Hangzhou section, the study reveals tensions between the "Authorized Heritage Discourse" and the lived experience of urban communities along the canal.

docs.title: Research Questions
docs.text:
• How does the authorized heritage discourse shape canal conservation policy and practice in urban China?
  • What happens when top-down heritage frameworks meet local urban development pressures?
  • Whose heritage is being conserved — and for whom?

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text: The Hangzhou case reveals a fundamental mismatch: official heritage discourse prioritizes the canal's material authenticity and historical monumentality, while local communities relate to the canal as a living infrastructure embedded in everyday urban life. This disconnect produces conservation outcomes that satisfy global heritage standards but alienate local populations.

docs.title: Outputs
docs.text: • Zhang, M. & Lenzer Jr, J. H. (2020). Mismatched canal conservation and the authorized heritage discourse in urban China: A case of the Hangzhou Section of the Grand Canal. International Journal of Heritage Studies, 26(2), 105–119. [DOI]

docs.title: Platform-Mediated Live Music and Urban Cultural Spaces
docs.text: 2018–2020 · Peking University Shenzhen Graduate School

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This project examines how digital platforms have reshaped live musical performance in China and, in turn, produced new forms of urban cultural space. Moving beyond conventional narratives of the "creative city," the research traces the concrete spatial practices through which platform-mediated music events reconfigure the use and meaning of urban venues.

docs.title: Research Questions
docs.text:
• How do digital platforms transform the organization, spatiality, and sociality of live music performance?
  • What new urban cultural spaces emerge from platform-mediated practices?
  • How do musicians, audiences, and venue operators navigate the platform economy?

docs.title: Methods
docs.text:
• Spatial data analysis of live music venues across Chinese cities using ArcGIS
  • Mixed-methods approach combining quantitative spatial analysis with qualitative inquiry

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text: Platform-mediated live music in China has generated new social practices that blur distinctions between formal and informal cultural economies. These practices produce distinct spatial patterns in the city — temporary clusters, pop-up venues, and hybrid commercial-cultural spaces that challenge conventional cultural planning frameworks.

docs.title: Outputs
docs.text: • Zhang, M. & Xiao, Z. (2023). Platform-mediated live musical performance in China: New social practices and urban cultural spaces. Geoforum, 140, 103723. [DOI]

docs.title: 首页
docs.text: 张梦珂  建筑与城市科学博士 · 瑞士洛桑联邦理工学院（EPFL）   我研究区域发展的政治经济学、边缘城市化与基础设施驱动的空间转型，尤其聚焦于中国。运用民族志与质性方法，探讨国家战略、大型赛事与金融化发展如何重塑地域、制度与日常生活。博士论文以2022年北京冬奥会驱动下崇礼延伸城市化的机制与实践为核心研究议题。  发表记录 研究 关于我

docs.title: 关于我
docs.text:
我是一名城乡规划、城市研究与人文地理交叉领域的研究者，关注区域发展的政治经济学、城乡规划理论与空间治理、边缘城市化与基础设施驱动的空间转型等议题。

我于瑞士洛桑联邦理工学院（EPFL）遗产、人类学与技术研究组（HAT）获得博士学位。博士论文《重塑山地：2022年北京冬奥会驱动下崇礼延伸城市化的机制与实践》以大型赛事与国家战略如何通过金融化、基础设施建设和空间社会转型重塑山地区域为核心议题。研究基于在河北崇礼累计七个月的民族志田野调查，深度访谈政府官员、国企管理者、滑雪度假区运营商及当地居民80余人次，并参与了瑞士国家科学基金会（SNSF）资助的国际跨学科研究项目。

我的研究兴趣集中于区域政治经济与空间治理、延伸城市化与边缘地区发展、基础设施与国家主导的区域转型、城乡融合与社会变迁、城市遗产与可持续更新等议题。在方法上，我主要运用质性研究与民族志方法——包括田野调查设计、半结构化访谈、参与式观察、政策与话语分析——并结合ArcGIS进行空间分析。

香港大学 — 香港 中国发展研究 硕士（Distinction）（2015–2016）

我和 Claude Code 搭了个网站 :calendar: 2026年5月29日 · :label: ClaudeCode 个人网站 学术主页

用 AI 辅助开发从零搭建中英双语学术主页的全记录——技术方案、协作流程、踩过的坑，以及让我意外的几个瞬间。目前已上线研究项目、发表记录、交互地图和本 Blog。

阅读全文 →

docs.title: 我和 Claude Code 搭了个网站
docs.text:
2026年5月29日 · 标签: ClaudeCode 个人网站 学术主页

想搭一个个人学术主页这件事，我想了大半年了。

和很多研究者一样，我想要的是：干净、中英双语、好维护。但每次一想到要动手就开始拖延——WordPress 太重，Notion 太锁，自己手写 HTML 又觉得是另一个周末的坑。

直到我试了试 Claude Code——就是你现在看到的这个工具。

docs.title: 开始
docs.text:
我往一个空文件夹里丢了份简历（Markdown 格式），然后问它："我们做个什么项目？"

它读了我的简历，知道我是做城乡研究的，然后给了四个建议。我选了 搭个人学术网站——实用、看得见成果、又是测试这个协作流程的好项目。

docs.title: 技术方案
docs.text:
我们选了 Material for MkDocs，理由很简单：

  • 内容用 Markdown — 我本来就大量用 .md 写东西，不需要学新的
  • 天然双语 — 英文和中文，一键切换。不是"以后再加"，而是一开始就有
  • 不锁平台 — 所有内容就是一堆 .md 文件，没有数据库，没有专有格式。将来想换工具，内容直接搬走
  • 免费部署 — GitHub Pages，零成本上线

docs.title: 协作流程
docs.text:
整个开发过程就是不断重复这个循环： 1. 我用中文说想要什么 2. Claude 给出方案和利弊分析 3. 我拍板或调整方向 4. Claude 写代码，我看着 5. 打开 localhost:8000 实时看效果 6. 我反馈，再改

从"空文件夹"到"一个能看的、双语的、带配色方案的网站"，只用了几个小时。

docs.title: 现在建了什么
docs.text:
截至目前，这个网站已经有了：

  • 主页 — 中英双语，干净的研究者简介和研究方向
  • 发表记录 — 6 篇期刊论文 + 2 个著作章节 + 在撰手稿，全部可点击 DOI
  • 研究项目 — 5 个独立项目页面，涵盖博士论文（崇礼延伸城市化）、北京冬奥会文化遗产、现场音乐与平台经济、单位制空间转型、大运河遗产保护
  • Blog — 就是这个页面。没有用 Material 的内置 blog 插件（它和中英双语插件不兼容，折腾了好一阵子），改用手写的 markdown 页面，简单可控
  • 交互地图 — Leaflet.js + OpenStreetMap，标注了崇礼、北京、杭州、深圳四个田野调查点，点击有说明
  • 暗色模式 — 一键切换，晚上看不刺眼

docs.title: 几个让我意外的点
docs.text:
• 它真的读懂了上下文。 我不需要反复解释"我是做什么的"。它读了我的简历，理解我的研究领域，能给出有针对性的建议。
  • 不用反复跳出去搜教程。 不用 Google "mkdocs i18n 怎么配置"——Claude 要么知道，要么现场读插件的源码。
  • 全过程在 Git 里。 所有内容、配置、设计决策都写在了 SITE-PLAN.md 里。项目是完整可迁移的。
  • 它还会记笔记。 我的偏好、项目配置、设计选择都存到了 memory 里，下次对话它还记得。
  • 它能读论文。 我把已发表的几篇 PDF 扔进文件夹，Claude 读完后自动提取了每个项目的研究问题、方法和核心发现，写了项目页面，我把关修改就行。

docs.title: Bug 也是学习过程
docs.text: 中间踩了不少坑： - Blog 插件和 i18n 插件不兼容——列表页一片空白。在 mkdocs serve 重启了好几次才发现问题。最后放弃插件，用手写的 markdown 页面反而更干净 - 中英切换有个 404 bug——英文版的 alternate link 配成了 /en/，但实际上英文是默认语言，应该指向 /。改一行就修好了 - 从中文主页翻译过去的英文页面，一些专用词翻译不准——比如把"研究组"翻成了"Laboratory"，"建筑与城市科学"翻成了"Architecture and Urban Sciences"。后来我把英文 CV 放进项目文件夹，Claude 对照着全部修正了

docs.title: 接下来
docs.text:
• 把网站部署上线
  • 继续在这里写 Blog
  • 可能加个自定义域名

docs.title: 小结
docs.text:
完美吗？不——有沟通不对位的时候，有 404 bug，有调了好几轮的 CSS，有插件兼容性搞了一小时的经历。但对于第一次用这种工具来说，这个节奏和质量是超出预期的。

如果你也是研究者，想要一个有模有样的网络门面但不想变成业余前端开发——这个工作流值得试试。

docs.title: 研究
docs.text: 我的研究聚焦于城市政治经济学、空间治理与延伸城市化的交叉领域，以中国为主要研究区域。我运用质性与民族志方法，探讨国家主导的发展如何重塑地域、制度与日常生活。

docs.title: 崇礼的延伸城市化
docs.text: **2020–2025** · 博士论文 · 瑞士洛桑联邦理工学院（EPFL） 2022年北京冬奥会如何将一个贫困山区县转变为中国首个奥运山地城市？本研究追踪了崇礼快速城市化背后的机制——金融、基础设施与社会层面的深刻变革。 延伸城市化地域商业模式国家发展城市主义

docs.title: 北京2022冬奥会文化遗产
docs.text: **2022–2024** · SNSF研究项目 作为瑞士国家科学基金会（SNSF）资助的国际跨学科研究团队的一员，探讨北京2022年冬奥会如何将文化遗产——从工业遗址到非物质文化遗产——用于国家主导的城市更新与国家品牌建设。 工业遗产大型赛事城市更新

docs.title: 平台中介的现场音乐与城市空间
docs.text: **2018–2020** · 北京大学深圳研究生院 数字平台如何重塑现场音乐表演，并由此创造新的城市文化空间？本研究对新生的文化经济形态进行了空间分析。 平台经济文化空间现场音乐

docs.title: 单位制社区的产权与空间演变
docs.text: **2018–2020** · 北京大学深圳研究生院 追踪市场改革以来，产权再分配如何驱动北京单位制社区的空间与制度转型。 产权单位制制度变迁

docs.title: 大运河遗产保护
docs.text: **2015–2016** · 硕士论文 · 香港大学 探讨官方遗产话语与地方现实在中国大运河保护中的错位——以杭州段为案例，追问"谁的遗产、为谁保护"。 权威遗产话语运河保护城市中国

docs.title: 北京2022冬奥会文化遗产
docs.text: 2022–2024 · SNSF研究项目 · EPFL

docs.title: 概述
docs.text:
作为瑞士国家科学基金会（SNSF）资助的国际研究项目"北京2022年冬奥会文化遗产的利用"的一部分，本研究探讨中国首届冬奥会如何将文化遗产——包括工业与非物质的——用于城市更新与国家品牌建设。

在一个横跨地理学、建筑学、人类学与社会学的跨学科团队中，项目考察了遗产在奥运盛事的宏大叙事中被定义、保存与再利用的创造性——有时是矛盾性的——方式。

docs.title: 研究焦点
docs.text:
• 中国政府如何采取一种国家主导的工业遗产方法，区别于西方市场驱动或公民社会主导的模式？
  • 在大型赛事背景下，遗产在资本积累与城市更新中扮演什么角色？
  • 不同层面的遗产叙事——地方的、国家的、全球的——如何在奥运驱动的发展中被协商？

docs.title: 核心发现
docs.text: 北京2022案例揭示了一种独特的国家主导模式：工业遗产保护同时服务于资本积累、城市更新与遗产保存——这三者在其他情境中常常面临张力。

docs.title: 成果
docs.text:
• Zhang, M. (2024). The state-led approach to industrial heritage in China's mega-events. Built Heritage, 8(30). [DOI]
  • Roux, H. & Zhang, M. (2026). "Mega-events and the creative use of cultural heritage." In P. Larsen & F. Graezer Bideau (Eds.), The Heritage, Creativity and Innovation Nexus. Routledge.

docs.title: 重塑山地：崇礼的延伸城市化
概述
docs.text:
本研究探讨2022年北京冬奥会如何催化了崇礼——一个距北京200公里、曾经是国家级贫困县的山地区域——转变为中国首个奥运山地城市。研究处于大型赛事、城市-区域发展与山地边缘地区城市化新范式的交叉点。

基于2021年末至2024年间累计七个月的民族志田野调查，本研究从三个相互关联的维度追踪崇礼的城市化进程：支撑山地开发的政治-经济制度配置、滑雪度假区作为关键社会基础设施的演变，以及奥运动能向乡村空间的延伸。

docs.title: 研究问题
docs.text:
• 在大型赛事背景下，哪些制度、金融和政治机制驱动了山地城市化？
  • 滑雪度假区如何同时作为城市消费者的"消费飞地"和当地社区的民生资源？
  • 奥运驱动的空间重构如何重新配置乡村经济网络、社会关系与居民生计？

docs.title: 研究方法
docs.text:
• 崇礼7个月民族志田野调查（2021–2024）
  • 80余次半结构化深度访谈，涵盖政府官员、国企管理者、度假区运营商及当地居民
  • 政策档案、规划文件与投资材料的纵向数据集

docs.title: 核心概念
docs.text:
国家发展城市主义 — 论文中引入的分析框架，用于刻画中国国家力量通过发展规划政策主导山地城市化的核心角色。

地域商业模式 — 将地域转化为可投资资产的制度、金融与政治安排，将土地租金生产与奥运驱动的金融化相链接。

docs.title: 成果
docs.text:
• Zhang, M. (2025). 博士论文, EPFL. [infoscience]
  • Theurillat, T. & Zhang, M. (2025). Financialized green state entrepreneurialism and the urbanization of mountains. IJURR. [DOI]
  • Zhang, M. 解构空间租金的关联性生产（在撰）
  • Zhang, M. 边缘地带的基础设施化（在撰）

docs.title: 单位制社区的产权与空间演变
docs.text: 2018–2020 · 北京大学深圳研究生院

docs.title: 概述
docs.text: 单位制社区是中国计划经济时代城市空间与社会组织的基本单元。市场改革以来，这些社区经历了深刻的转型。本项目追踪产权再分配如何重塑北京的单位制社区空间、制度与社区关系。

docs.title: 研究问题
docs.text:
• 产权再分配如何驱动单位制社区的空间演变？
  • 哪些制度机制在产权变化与空间结果之间起中介作用？
  • 居民与单位如何协商这些社会主义遗产空间的转型？

docs.title: 实证研究
docs.text: 在北京开展的田野研究将单位制社区视为更广泛的中国城市转型缩影。通过追踪具体社区的空间与制度变迁轨迹，研究揭示了社会主义计划经济的遗产如何持续塑造当代城市形态。

docs.title: 成果
docs.text:
• Zhang, M., Zhang, T., Xiao, Z., & Chai, Y. (2021). Property rights redistribution and the spatial evolution of the Chinese danwei compound. Journal of Housing and the Built Environment, 36(4), 1585–1602. [DOI]
  • Xiao, Z., Liu, T., Chai, Y., & Zhang, M. (2020). Corporate-run society: The practice of the danwei system in Beijing during the planned economy period. Sustainability, 12(4), 1338. [DOI]
  • Xiao, Z., Liu, T., Chai, Y., & Zhang, M. (2020). Rethinking the spatial prototype and operational organization of the Chinese danwei system. In S. Jacoby & J. Cheng (Eds.), The Socio-spatial Design of Community and Governance (pp. 79–86). Springer.

docs.title: 概述
docs.text: 本项目源自我在香港大学的硕士论文，探讨官方遗产框架与中国大运河——全长逾1700公里的联合国教科文组织世界遗产——保护实践中的错位。以杭州段为案例，研究揭示了"权威遗产话语"与运河沿岸城市社区居民日常体验之间的张力。

docs.title: 研究问题
docs.text:
• 权威遗产话语如何形塑中国大运河保护政策与实践？
  • 当自上而下的遗产框架遭遇地方城市发展压力时，会发生什么？
  • 谁的遗产被保护——又为谁而保护？

docs.title: 核心发现
docs.text: 杭州案例揭示了一种根本性的错位：官方遗产话语强调运河的物质真实性与历史纪念碑性，而当地社区则将运河视为嵌入日常生活的生活化基础设施。这种断裂产生的保护成果，虽满足全球遗产标准，却疏离了当地居民。

docs.title: 成果
docs.text: • Zhang, M. & Lenzer Jr, J. H. (2020). Mismatched canal conservation and the authorized heritage discourse in urban China: A case of the Hangzhou Section of the Grand Canal. International Journal of Heritage Studies, 26(2), 105–119. [DOI]

docs.title: 平台中介的现场音乐与城市文化空间
docs.text: 2018–2020 · 北京大学深圳研究生院

docs.title: 概述
docs.text: 本项目探讨数字平台如何重塑中国的现场音乐表演，进而催生新的城市文化空间形态。超越传统的"创意城市"叙事，本研究追踪平台中介的音乐活动如何通过具体的空间实践重构城市场所的使用方式与文化意义。

docs.title: 研究问题
docs.text:
• 数字平台如何转变现场音乐表演的组织方式、空间性与社会性？
  • 平台中介的实践催生了哪些新的城市文化空间？
  • 音乐人、观众与场地运营者如何在平台经济中博弈？

docs.title: 研究方法
docs.text:
• 基于ArcGIS的中国城市现场音乐演出场所空间数据分析
  • 定量空间分析与质性调查相结合的混合方法

docs.title: 核心发现
docs.text: 平台中介的现场音乐在中国催生了新的社会实践，模糊了正式与非正式文化经济之间的界限。这些实践在城市中产生了独特的空间模式——临时集群、快闪场地、混合型商业-文化空间——这些都对传统的文化规划框架构成了挑战。

docs.title: 成果
docs.text: • Zhang, M. & Xiao, Z. (2023). Platform-mediated live musical performance in China: New social practices and urban cultural spaces. Geoforum, 140, 103723. [DOI]
